responses.content: I think the main idea of this is that she stood up for her self because she is a role model  of other people. Here is more proof that people followed her footsteps “In October 1955, several months after Claudette was convicted, Mary Louise Smith, an eighteen-year-old black girl, was arrested when she refused to move to the back of the bus so a white woman could take her seat.” This shows that not just she didnt stand up for black people but she encouraged other black people to stand up for others.

questions.question: In the Conclusion box, write a concluding paragraph (3-4 sentences) that wraps up your comparison and explains why these stories matter.

responses.content: I think the main idea from oFreedom’s Daughters that Barbara Johns showed the reader is that she was not scared to become broke, killed, or be imprisoned if it meant that everyone would have equal rights. I know this because this is what is says in the story “ that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community”. This evidence shows that Barbra was not scared to be broke, imprisoned or worse.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response is based on a misunderstanding of the text. The student claims that Barbara Johns was not scared to become broke, killed, or imprisoned, but the text does not mention Barbara Johns being afraid of being broke, killed, or imprisoned. The quote used is about the NAACP's requirements for helping the students, not about Barbara's personal feelings or fears. Therefore, the response demonstrates a fundamental misreading of the text.

responses.content: I think the main idea from oFreedom’s Daughters that Barbara Johns showed the reader is that she was not scared to become broke, killed, or be imprisoned if it meant that everyone would have equal rights. I know this because this is what is says in the story “ that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community”. This evidence shows that Barbara was not scared to be broke, imprisoned or worse. The NAACP explained the risks of what fighting for equal rights would look like.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows a fundamental misunderstanding of the text. The student claims that Barbara Johns was not scared to become broke, killed, or imprisoned, but the text does not state that Barbara Johns faced or accepted these specific risks. The quote about 'going for broke' refers to the NAACP's willingness to help only if the students were ready to push for full desegregation, not that Barbara was unafraid of being broke, killed, or imprisoned. The student's evidence is misinterpreted and does not accurately reflect the events or main idea from the excerpt.

responses.content:
I think the main idea from Freedom’s Daughters that Barbara Johns showed the reader is that she was not scared to become broke or get in trouble if it meant that everyone would have equal rights. 
“The time had come, she said, for dramatic action. Joined by the other student organizers, she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” Her ability to go on strike for equal rights shows that she was not afraid of what would happen to her.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a clear topic sentence about Barbara Johns' bravery and one piece of evidence from the text (the quote about calling for dramatic action and organizing a strike). The explanation connects the evidence to the main idea. However, the response only provides one piece of evidence and one explanation, when two are required for a fully correct answer. Therefore, it fits the 'partially-correct-high' category.

questions.question: Review your complete comparative essay one final time. Make any last edits to strengthen your analysis, improve your evidence, or clarify your comparisons before submitting for your final grade. Make these edits in the Final Draft box.

responses.content:
Freedom Walkers and Freedom’s Daughters is a story about 2 girls who are in high school who both fought for black people’s rights. One thing that is similar about them is that they both risked their lives and the chance of going broke for their rights. Something that is different about their stories is that Claudette worked alone and Barbara worked with a group of students. I think the main idea of this is that she stood up for her self because she is a role model  of other people. Claudette's actions showed bravery because int the story it says  “two months later, on December 1, 1955, another black woman boarded a city bus and found an empty seat just behind the white section. She was Rosa Parks.” In this paragraph is shows proof that she was a role model because other people followed her footsteps. Here is more proof that people followed her footsteps “In October 1955, several months after Claudette was convicted, Mary Louise Smith, an eighteen-year-old black girl, was arrested when she refused to move to the back of the bus so a white woman could take her seat.” This shows that not just she didnt stand up for black people but she encouraged other black people to stand up for others. I think the main idea from Freedom’s Daughters that Barbara Johns showed the reader is that she was not scared to become broke or get in trouble if it meant that everyone would have equal rights. 
“The time had come, she said, for dramatic action. Joined by the other student organizers, she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” Her ability to go on strike for equal rights shows that she was not afraid of what would happen to her. In the end  I learned that being brave can and fight for your rights will reward you. By being brave like Barbara and  going on strike we can fight against desegregation. As for Claudette, she was brave and refused to give up her seat on the bus because it was her constitutional right and should have been rewarded equal rights. These stories matter because it is important to follow the lesson of being brave and fighting for your rights no matter what.
responses.bucket_rationale: The paragraph contains several grammar and spelling mistakes, such as 'int' instead of 'in,' 'didnt' instead of 'didn't,' and 'her self' instead of 'herself.' These errors go beyond mere style or phrasing suggestions, thus classifying the response as 'incorrect.'
